- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).

content.text:
Good news: your hardware is well suited for this. RTX 3080 Ti, i7-12700K, 31 GB RAM, and the proprietary NVIDIA driver is already loaded. Two things stand in the way:

1. **The `multilib` repo is disabled.** Steam is a 32-bit application, so it can't be installed until you enable it in `/etc/pacman.conf`.
2. **No 32-bit graphics libraries.** You have `nvidia-utils` and `vulkan-icd-loader`, but games need the `lib32-` counterparts too.

One thing worth flagging before I touch anything: your root partition has only **32 GB free**, while `/home` has **763 GB**. Steam installs games to `~/.local/share/Steam` by default, which lands on `/home` — so that works out fine as-is.

Let me check what you'd like included.

questions.question: Which game stores/launchers do you want set up?

options.label: Steam (Recommended)
options.description: The essential one. Includes Proton, which runs most Windows games on Linux with no extra config. Pulls in multilib + lib32 NVIDIA libraries.

options.label: Heroic Games Launcher
options.description: Free/open-source launcher for Epic Games Store, GOG, and Amazon Prime Gaming libraries.

options.label: Lutris
options.description: Universal launcher with install scripts for non-Steam games, emulators, Battle.net, EA App, etc. More manual than Heroic.

options.label: Bottles
options.description: Friendly Wine prefix manager for running arbitrary Windows apps and games outside any store.

questions.question: Want the usual performance and monitoring extras?

options.label: MangoHud + GameMode (Recommended)
options.description: MangoHud shows an in-game FPS/temps/frametime overlay. GameMode applies CPU governor and priority tweaks while a game runs.

options.label: ProtonUp-Qt
options.description: GUI to install community Proton builds like Proton-GE, which fix compatibility for many games Valve's Proton struggles with.

options.label: Gamescope
options.description: Valve's micro-compositor. Genuinely useful on Hyprland for fixing fullscreen, resolution scaling, and HDR issues in games.

content.text: `/etc/pacman.conf` is root-owned, so this needs sudo — it may prompt for your password.

input.command: sudo cp /etc/pacman.conf /etc/pacman.conf.bak && sudo sed -i '/^#\[multilib\]$/,/^#Include/ s/^#//' /etc/pacman.conf && echo "--- result ---" && grep -n -A 1 "^\[multilib\]" /etc/pacman.conf

content.text: Sudo needs an interactive terminal, which I don't have — so I'll write the setup as a script and you can run it yourself. Let me put it together.

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Gaming setup for Arch + NVIDIA RTX 3080 Ti + Hyprland/Wayland
set -euo pipefail

echo "==> 1/4  Enabling the multilib repository"
if grep -q '^\[multilib\]' /etc/pacman.conf; then
    echo "    multilib is already enabled, skipping."
else
    sudo cp /etc/pacman.conf "/etc/pacman.conf.bak.$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)"
    sudo sed -i '/^#\[multilib\]$/,/^#Include/ s/^#//' /etc/pacman.conf
    grep -A 1 '^\[multilib\]' /etc/pacman.conf
fi

echo
echo "==> 2/4  Refreshing package databases"
sudo pacman -Syu --noconfirm

echo
echo "==> 3/4  Installing Steam, 32-bit graphics libraries, and tools"
# lib32-nvidia-utils  : 32-bit NVIDIA userspace driver (works with nvidia-open)
# lib32-vulkan-icd-loader : 32-bit Vulkan loader, required by Proton/DXVK
# lib32-mesa          : 32-bit OpenGL fallback, pulled in by several games
# gamemode/mangohud   : need both 64- and 32-bit versions to hook every game
# gamescope           : Valve micro-compositor, fixes fullscreen on Hyprland
sudo pacman -S --needed \
    steam \
    lib32-nvidia-utils \
    lib32-vulkan-icd-loader \
    lib32-mesa \
    vulkan-tools \
    gamemode lib32-gamemode \
    mangohud lib32-mangohud \
    gamescope

echo
echo "==> 4/4  Installing ProtonUp-Qt from the AUR"
yay -S --needed protonup-qt

echo
echo "==> Done. Verifying the Vulkan stack:"
vulkaninfo --summary 2>/dev/null | grep -E "GPU id|deviceName|driverName" || \
    echo "    (run 'vulkaninfo --summary' manually to inspect)"

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Gaming setup for Arch + NVIDIA RTX 3080 Ti + Hyprland/Wayland
set -euo pipefail

echo "==> 1/4  Enabling the multilib repository"
if grep -q '^\[multilib\]' /etc/pacman.conf; then
    echo "    multilib is already enabled, skipping."
else
    sudo cp /etc/pacman.conf "/etc/pacman.conf.bak.$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)"
    sudo sed -i '/^#\[multilib\]$/,/^#Include/ s/^#//' /etc/pacman.conf
    grep -A 1 '^\[multilib\]' /etc/pacman.conf
fi

echo
echo "==> 2/4  Refreshing package databases"
sudo pacman -Syu --noconfirm

echo
echo "==> 3/4  Installing Steam, 32-bit graphics libraries, and tools"
# lib32-nvidia-utils  : 32-bit NVIDIA userspace driver (works with nvidia-open)
# lib32-vulkan-icd-loader : 32-bit Vulkan loader, required by Proton/DXVK
# lib32-mesa          : 32-bit OpenGL fallback, pulled in by several games
# gamemode/mangohud   : need both 64- and 32-bit versions to hook every game
# gamescope           : Valve micro-compositor, fixes fullscreen on Hyprland
sudo pacman -S --needed \
    steam \
    lib32-nvidia-utils \
    lib32-vulkan-icd-loader \
    lib32-mesa \
    vulkan-tools \
    gamemode lib32-gamemode \
    mangohud lib32-mangohud \
    gamescope

echo
echo "==> 4/4  Installing ProtonUp-Qt from the AUR"
yay -S --needed protonup-qt

echo
echo "==> Done. Verifying the Vulkan stack:"
vulkaninfo --summary 2>/dev/null | grep -E "GPU id|deviceName|driverName" || \
    echo "    (run 'vulkaninfo --summary' manually to inspect)"

content.text:
Script's ready. Run it by pasting this into your prompt — the `!` prefix runs it in your terminal so the sudo password prompt actually works, and the output comes back into our conversation:

```
! bash /tmp/claude-1000/-home-tunc/9e45da26-8187-4127-986f-e6a458a19b46/scratchpad/setup-gaming.sh
```

Two things you'll be asked during the run:

- **A provider choice** for `lib32-vulkan-driver` or similar — pick the `lib32-nvidia-utils` option, not the Mesa one.
- **Confirmation from `yay`** on the AUR build for ProtonUp-Qt.

Here's what it does and why:

**Enables `multilib`** (with a timestamped backup of `pacman.conf`), because Steam is a 32-bit binary and is unavailable without it.

**Installs Steam plus the 32-bit driver stack.** `lib32-nvidia-utils` is the piece that actually matters — you have the 64-bit driver, but Proton games are frequently 32-bit and will fail without its counterpart. `lib32-vulkan-icd-loader` is what DXVK uses to translate DirectX to Vulkan.

**Adds GameMode, MangoHud, and Gamescope.** Gamescope is worth calling out for your setup specifically: fullscreen and resolution scaling in games are a known rough edge on Hyprland, and running a game inside Gamescope sidesteps most of it.

Once it finishes, open Steam, then go to **Settings → Compatibility → Enable Steam Play for all other titles**. That's the switch that lets you install Windows games; it's off by default and nothing else here substitutes for it.

To use the extras, set a game's launch options (right-click the game → Properties) to something like:

```
gamemoderun mangohud %command%
```

Or, if a game misbehaves in fullscreen:

```
gamescope -f -W 2560 -H 1440 -- gamemoderun %command%
```

A note on disk: Steam will install games to `~/.local/share/Steam` on `/home`, where you have 763 GB. Your root partition has only 32 GB free, so if you ever add a Steam library folder, keep it under `/home`.

Once you've got MangoHud configured the way you like, tell me and I'll add `~/.config/MangoHud` to your `arch-tunc` backup allowlist — package installs are captured automatically, but config files only sync if they're explicitly listed.
